questions.question: Order the 7 steps of the experimental design checklist from first to last.
items: Choose the design (between or within subjects)
correct_order: Choose the design (between or within subjects)
questions.explanation: The checklist runs research question, hypothesis, IV/DV, conditions, design, control confounds, then plan analysis. Each step builds on the previous one, ending with the analysis plan that the design itself dictates.

questions.question: Order the steps of the null-hypothesis-testing logic from first to last.
items: Decide: reject or fail to reject H0
correct_order: Decide: reject or fail to reject H0
questions.explanation: You start by assuming H0, then collect data, compute a statistic, derive its p-value, and finally decide whether to reject or fail to reject H0. You can never prove H0, only fail to reject it.

questions.question: Order these design choices by the sample size typically required, from smallest to largest total N (course practical values).
questions.explanation: A within-subjects design reuses participants, needing about 20 total for the course, whereas a between-subjects design needs roughly twice that, about 40 total (20 per condition).

questions.question: Order these steps for planning the student example study (noise level on mind-wandering during reading) as they follow the design checklist.
items: RQ: does noise level affect mind-wandering while reading?
IV = noise level, DV = mind-wandering during reading
correct_order: RQ: does noise level affect mind-wandering while reading?
IV = noise level, DV = mind-wandering during reading
questions.explanation: Following the checklist, the research question comes first, then the hypothesis, then naming the IV and DV, and then selecting the between-subjects design before controlling confounds and planning analysis.

questions.question: Order these steps for using G*Power to plan a study, from first to last.
items: Read off the required sample size N
correct_order: Read off the required sample size N
questions.explanation: G*Power computes required N from the inputs: alpha, desired power, and expected effect size are specified first, and the tool then returns the sample size needed to achieve that power.
